edges.description: Failed epithelial copper export lowers circulating copper and ceruloplasmin availability.
Inadequate copper transfer across brain barriers limits neuronal copper delivery during development.
ATP7A inactivation can also cause paradoxical intracellular copper accumulation and mitochondrial redox injury.
edges.target: Mitochondrial redox imbalance from copper accumulation

edges.description: Reduced systemic copper availability is measured clinically as low serum copper.
Inadequate copper availability reduces circulating ceruloplasmin.
Peripheral copper deficiency reduces activity of the copper-dependent connective-tissue enzyme lysyl oxidase.
edges.target: Lysyl oxidase deficiency and connective tissue fragility

edges.description: Inadequate copper delivery to hair-shaft and pigment enzymes causes the characteristic kinky and hypopigmented hair phenotype.
edges.target: Hair-shaft keratinization and pigmentation enzyme dysfunction

edges.description: Brain copper deficiency impairs copper-dependent enzymes needed for catecholamine synthesis and mitochondrial energy metabolism.
Neuronal copper deficiency lowers seizure threshold and contributes to epileptic manifestations.
Impaired energy metabolism and catecholamine synthesis disrupt normal brain growth and development.
Impaired copper-dependent neuronal metabolism contributes to irreversible brain atrophy.
Neurologic injury from cuproenzyme deficiency manifests clinically as hypotonia.
Early neurologic dysfunction and systemic copper deficiency contribute to poor growth.
Dopamine-beta-hydroxylase deficiency increases diagnostic catecholamine metabolite ratios.
Defective extracellular-matrix cross-linking weakens vessel walls and alters vascular architecture.
edges.source: Lysyl oxidase deficiency and connective tissue fragility

edges.description: Weak collagen and elastin cross-linking contributes to hernias, skin laxity, and skeletal complications.
edges.source: Lysyl oxidase deficiency and connective tissue fragility

edges.description: Vessel-wall matrix weakness produces tortuous intracranial arteries.
Venous wall weakness produces fusiform internal jugular vein dilatation.
Defective collagen and elastin cross-linking causes loose, redundant, wrinkled skin.
Weak bladder-wall connective tissue predisposes to diverticula.
Skeletal connective-tissue and mineralization abnormalities include wormian bones.
Fragile connective tissue and vessels predispose to subdural hemorrhage.
Hair-shaft structural disruption produces twisted, brittle pili torti.
edges.source: Hair-shaft keratinization and pigmentation enzyme dysfunction

edges.description: Impaired pigment enzyme activity produces hypopigmented fair hair.
edges.source: Hair-shaft keratinization and pigmentation enzyme dysfunction

nodes.description: AAV-mediated ATP7A gene delivery remains investigational but has shown preclinical rescue in Menkes mouse models, particularly when combined with copper histidinate.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebrospinal Fluid-Directed rAAV9-rsATP7A Plus Subcutaneous Copper Histidinate Advance Survival and Outcomes in a Menkes Disease Mouse Model.
evidence.snippet: Based on these findings, and the absence of a large animal model, we propose cerebrospinal fluid-directed rAAV9-rsATP7A gene therapy plus subcutaneous copper histidinate as a potential therapeutic approach to cure or ameliorate Menkes disease.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal diagnosis and treatment of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Menkes disease is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder of infancy caused by diverse mutations in a copper-transport gene, ATP7A.

evidence.reference_title: ATP7A‐related copper transport disorders: A systematic review and definition of the clinical subtypes
evidence.snippet: Truncating mutations are frequently associated with CMD, in contrast with splice site and intronic mutations which are more prevalent in OHS.

evidence.reference_title: ATP7A / Menkes disease (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ATP7A | HGNC:869 | Menkes disease | MONDO:0010651 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: Some ATP7A variants preserve partial ATPase activity or some correctly spliced transcript, producing residual copper transport that can modify severity and response to early copper treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal diagnosis and treatment of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Affected newborns who have mutations that do not completely abrogate ATP7A function may be especially responsive to early copper treatment.

evidence.reference_title: ATP7A‐related copper transport disorders: A systematic review and definition of the clinical subtypes
evidence.snippet: Truncating mutations are frequently associated with CMD, in contrast with splice site and intronic mutations which are more prevalent in OHS.

nodes.description: Nonsense, frameshift, splice-site, and copy-number ATP7A variants can severely reduce or abolish copper transport, creating the classic early infantile Menkes pathograph that begins with failed epithelial copper export.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted next generation sequencing for newborn screening of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: These included eight copy number variants (six large deletions, two large duplications (Fig. 1), four small insertion/deletions causing translational reading frameshifts, as well as three nonsense, three splice junction, and four missense variants.

evidence.reference_title: ATP7A‐related copper transport disorders: A systematic review and definition of the clinical subtypes
evidence.snippet: Truncating mutations are frequently associated with CMD, in contrast with splice site and intronic mutations which are more prevalent in OHS.

nodes.description: ATP7A dysfunction impairs copper movement from the cytosol into the secretory pathway and prevents directional copper export across polarized epithelial barriers. The result is low circulating copper availability with copper retention in intestinal and renal epithelia and inadequate delivery to peripheral tissues and the brain.

evidence.reference_title: Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: ATP7A is an energy dependent transmembrane protein, which is involved in the delivery of copper to the secreted copper enzymes and in the export of surplus copper from cells.

evidence.reference_title: The Activity of Menkes Disease Protein ATP7A Is Essential for Redox Balance in Mitochondria.
evidence.snippet: In cells, ATP7A inactivation disrupts copper transport from the cytosol into the secretory pathway.

nodes.description: Tortuous and elongated intracranial arteries reflect defective elastin cross-linking from lysyl oxidase deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Imaging findings of Menkes disease, a radiographic mimic of abusive trauma.
evidence.snippet: The classic radiographic findings seen in Menke's disease have been documented and include wormian bones, diaphyseal and metaphyseal bone fractures, subdural hematomas, and tortuous intracranial arteries

nodes.description: Diverticula of the urinary bladder wall arise from connective tissue weakness due to impaired elastin and collagen cross-linking.

evidence.reference_title: Bladder diverticula and Menkes' syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Multiple unusual diverticula of the bladder were observed in 3 of 4 children with Menkes' syndrome.

nodes.description: ATP7A-dependent copper transfer across brain barriers is inadequate, limiting copper availability to developing neurons and CNS cuproenzymes during the brief presymptomatic neonatal window.

evidence.reference_title: Catecholamine metabolites affected by the copper-dependent enzyme dopamine-beta-hydroxylase provide sensitive biomarkers for early diagnosis of menkes disease and viral-mediated ATP7A gene therapy.
evidence.snippet: Both conditions are linked to a biochemical phenotype (low copper levels in the blood and a copper deficiency in the brain) that denotes abnormal copper metabolism.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebral atrophy develops during infancy as a consequence of copper-dependent enzyme deficiency and impaired neuronal energy metabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted next generation sequencing for newborn screening of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: At birth, affected Menkes infants appear healthy and typically develop normally for 6 to 10 weeks before seizures, failure to thrive, irreversible brain atrophy, and stalled neurodevelopment ensue.

nodes.description: Reduced extracellular-matrix cross-linking weakens skin, bladder wall, cranial sutures, bones, and bridging vessels, producing the connective tissue phenotype branch of Menkes disease.

evidence.reference_title: Neck masses due to internal jugular vein phlebectasia: Frequency in Menkes disease and literature review of 85 pediatric subjects.
evidence.snippet: This feature and other connective tissue abnormalities appear to be consequences of deficient activity of lysyl oxidase, a copper-dependent enzyme.

nodes.description: Early subcutaneous copper histidinate partially bypasses the intestinal copper-transport defect and improves survival and neurodevelopment most effectively when started in the presymptomatic neonatal window.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal diagnosis and treatment of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Among 12 newborns with positive screening tests who were treated early with copper, survival at a median follow-up of 4.6 years was 92%, as compared with 13% at a median follow-up of 1.8 years for a historical control group of 15 late-diagnosis and late-treatment patients.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopment and brain growth in classic Menkes disease is influenced by age and symptomatology at initiation of copper treatment.
evidence.snippet: We conclude that early copper histidine for Menkes disease is safe and efficacious, with treatment outcomes influenced by the timing of intervention, and ATP7A mutation.

nodes.description: Brain copper deficiency compromises copper-dependent enzymes, especially dopamine-beta-hydroxylase and cytochrome c oxidase. This causes abnormal catecholamine biosynthesis, impaired oxidative phosphorylation, and progressive neurodevelopmental injury with seizures, hypotonia, and brain atrophy.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal diagnosis and treatment of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Exploiting the deficiency of a copper enzyme, dopamine-beta-hydroxylase, we prospectively evaluated the diagnostic usefulness of plasma neurochemical levels, assessed the clinical effect of early detection, and investigated the molecular bases for treatment outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: The Activity of Menkes Disease Protein ATP7A Is Essential for Redox Balance in Mitochondria.
evidence.snippet: Thus, ATP7A activity protects mitochondria from excessive copper entry, which is deleterious to redox buffers.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted next generation sequencing for newborn screening of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: At birth, affected Menkes infants appear healthy and typically develop normally for 6 to 10 weeks before seizures, failure to thrive, irreversible brain atrophy, and stalled neurodevelopment ensue.

nodes.description: Wrinkled, redundant, inelastic skin results from reduced lysyl oxidase activity and deficient collagen and elastin cross-linking.

evidence.reference_title: In utero copper treatment for Menkes disease associated with a severe ATP7A mutation.
evidence.snippet: His skin was dry and wrinkled with a post-mature quality, and loose and redundant on the back and trunk.

nodes.description: Poor growth accompanies neurologic decline, feeding difficulty, and multisystem copper deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopment and brain growth in classic Menkes disease is influenced by age and symptomatology at initiation of copper treatment.
evidence.snippet: Untreated affected individuals suffer failure to thrive and neurodevelopmental delays that usually commence at 6-8 weeks of age.

nodes.description: Hair hypopigmentation results from impaired copper delivery to tyrosinase, the copper-dependent melanin biosynthesis enzyme.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroimaging in Menkes Disease.
evidence.snippet: These patients can present with failure to thrive, severe psychomotor retardation, seizures and hypopigmented hair, which is characteristic of this condition.

nodes.description: Copper-dependent hair and pigment biology is disrupted, producing brittle twisted hair shafts and hair hypopigmentation.
nodes.id: Hair-shaft keratinization and pigmentation enzyme dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Progressive neurodegeneration and connective tissue disturbances, together with the peculiar 'kinky' hair are the main manifestations.

nodes.description: Generalized hypotonia is a common early sign of central and peripheral nervous system involvement.

evidence.reference_title: In utero copper treatment for Menkes disease associated with a severe ATP7A mutation.
evidence.snippet: Despite this very early intervention with copper, the infant showed hypotonia, developmental delay, and electroencephalographic abnormalities and died of respiratory failure at 5.5 months of age.

nodes.description: Fusiform dilatation of the internal jugular vein occurs in approximately 14% of Menkes disease patients and can present as neck masses.

evidence.reference_title: Neck masses due to internal jugular vein phlebectasia: Frequency in Menkes disease and literature review of 85 pediatric subjects.
evidence.snippet: Our prospective results suggest that IJP occurs in approximately 14% (9/66) of Menkes disease patients and appears to be clinically benign with no specific medical or surgical actionability.

nodes.description: Copper deficiency reduces lysyl oxidase activity, weakening collagen and elastin cross-linking in connective tissues and vessel walls. This produces tissue laxity, vascular tortuosity and phlebectasia, hernias, and skeletal fragility.
nodes.id: Lysyl oxidase deficiency and connective tissue fragility

evidence.reference_title: Neck masses due to internal jugular vein phlebectasia: Frequency in Menkes disease and literature review of 85 pediatric subjects.
evidence.snippet: This feature and other connective tissue abnormalities appear to be consequences of deficient activity of lysyl oxidase, a copper-dependent enzyme.

nodes.description: In cells lacking functional ATP7A, copper accumulates in mitochondria and disrupts glutathione-based redox buffering. This renders cells highly sensitive to oxidative stress and contributes to tissue injury, particularly in renal epithelia where copper paradoxically accumulates.
nodes.id: Mitochondrial redox imbalance from copper accumulation

evidence.reference_title: The Activity of Menkes Disease Protein ATP7A Is Essential for Redox Balance in Mitochondria.
evidence.snippet: Redox misbalance does not significantly affect mitochondrion morphology or the activity of respiratory complex IV but markedly increases cell sensitivity to even mild glutathione depletion, resulting in loss of cell viability.

evidence.reference_title: The Activity of Menkes Disease Protein ATP7A Is Essential for Redox Balance in Mitochondria.
evidence.snippet: Mitochondrial redox misbalance could significantly contribute to pathologies associated with ATP7A inactivation in tissues with paradoxical accumulation of copper (i.e. renal epithelia).

nodes.description: Progressive neurodevelopmental impairment emerges in early infancy after a brief normal neonatal period.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopment and brain growth in classic Menkes disease is influenced by age and symptomatology at initiation of copper treatment.
evidence.snippet: Untreated affected individuals suffer failure to thrive and neurodevelopmental delays that usually commence at 6-8 weeks of age.

nodes.description: Characteristic brittle, twisted kinky hair reflects impaired copper delivery to hair-shaft and pigmentation enzymes.

evidence.reference_title: Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Progressive neurodegeneration and connective tissue disturbances, together with the peculiar 'kinky' hair are the main manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Catecholamine metabolites affected by the copper-dependent enzyme dopamine-beta-hydroxylase provide sensitive biomarkers for early diagnosis of menkes disease and viral-mediated ATP7A gene therapy.
evidence.snippet: Partial deficiency of dopamine-beta-hydroxylase is a biochemical hallmark of this illness due to the normal role of ATP7A in delivery of copper as an enzymatic cofactor.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal diagnosis and treatment of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Exploiting the deficiency of a copper enzyme, dopamine-beta-hydroxylase, we prospectively evaluated the diagnostic usefulness of plasma neurochemical levels, assessed the clinical effect of early detection, and investigated the molecular bases for treatment outcomes.

nodes.description: Seizures often emerge during early infancy as neurodegeneration and brain copper deficiency progress.

evidence.reference_title: Targeted next generation sequencing for newborn screening of Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: At birth, affected Menkes infants appear healthy and typically develop normally for 6 to 10 weeks before seizures, failure to thrive, irreversible brain atrophy, and stalled neurodevelopment ensue.

evidence.reference_title: In utero copper treatment for Menkes disease associated with a severe ATP7A mutation.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals manifest low copper and ceruloplasmin levels in plasma or serum, as well as in cerebrospinal fluid.

evidence.reference_title: In utero copper treatment for Menkes disease associated with a severe ATP7A mutation.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals manifest low copper and ceruloplasmin levels in plasma or serum, as well as in cerebrospinal fluid.

nodes.description: Fragile bridging veins due to lysyl oxidase deficiency predispose to subdural hemorrhage, which may be mistaken for non-accidental injury.

evidence.reference_title: Massive subdural haematomas in Menkes disease mimicking shaken baby syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of Menkes disease complicated by progressive macrocephaly following the development of massive subdural haematomas.

nodes.description: Failed ATP7A-dependent epithelial copper export causes low circulating copper and ceruloplasmin. Systemic copper deficiency limits copper delivery to secretory-pathway cuproenzymes in peripheral tissues.

evidence.reference_title: In utero copper treatment for Menkes disease associated with a severe ATP7A mutation.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals manifest low copper and ceruloplasmin levels in plasma or serum, as well as in cerebrospinal fluid.

nodes.description: Weak elastin and collagen cross-linking in vessel walls leads to tortuous intracranial arteries and benign internal jugular vein phlebectasia.

evidence.reference_title: Neck masses due to internal jugular vein phlebectasia: Frequency in Menkes disease and literature review of 85 pediatric subjects.
evidence.snippet: We previously identified internal jugular vein phlebectasia (IJP) in four Menkes disease subjects. This feature and other connective tissue abnormalities appear to be consequences of deficient activity of lysyl oxidase, a copper-dependent enzyme.

nodes.description: Supernumerary intrasutural bones in the cranium reflect abnormal skeletal mineralization and connective tissue development.

evidence.reference_title: Long-term skeletal findings in Menkes disease.
evidence.snippet: Skeletal findings in infants with Menkes disease, the most characteristic of which are metaphyseal spurs, long-bone fractures and wormian bones, have been widely reported.
